content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To     : File                                          Date  : Mar. 22, 1977


                                                       77-23-262

From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Ken Finney

Subject: Item No. 6 on the Commission's March agenda.

     The Commission determined at its meeting on March 3, 1977,
that the value of the gift in each of the circumstances
referred to in my memorandum of February 28, 1977 (a copy
of which is attached), is the amount that an individual
normally would pay to receive the benefits provided at
the event. Accordingly, if the charity fundraiser serves
a dinner worth approximately $15, the value of the gift
to the public official is $15. If the charity fundraiser
serves a dinner and has a well-known celebrity provide enter-
tainment, the value of the gift to the public official is
the amount that a person normally would pay to attend a
dinner at which the celebrity appeared. For example, if
Frank Sinatra sings at the event and it normally costs $50
to see Frank Sinatra in Las Vegas and have dinner, the
value of the gift is $50. To the extent that it is diffi-
cult to ascertain a precise value for the gift under the
foregoing circumstances, a good faith estimate by the of-
ficial will suffice. See Opinion requested by Kenneth Cory,
1 FPPC Ops. 153, 154 (No. 75-094B, October 23, 1975.)

KF:jo

Attachment

State of California

Memorandum

To   : Members of the Commission                          Date : Feb. 28, 1977

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Kenneth Finney

Subject: Item No. 6 on the Commission's March agenda.

     The organizers of a charity fundraiser for which tickets
are being sold for $100 each invite a public official to
attend and do not charge him for a ticket.

     Mr. A. purchases two tickets at $100 each to the afore-
mentioned fundraiser. He then gives one of the tickets
to a public official as a gift.

     The issue for discussion is how to value the amount of the
gift in each of the foregoing situations.

     The staff unanimously believes that the value of the gift
in each situation is $100 since that is what it would have
cost the recipient official to attend the charity fund-
raiser if he had been required to purchase a ticket. The
Chairman, on the other hand, believes that the value of
the gift is the value of the items actually received by
the public official at the fundraiser. In other words,
the Chairman would place a value on the meal, the enter-
tainment and whatever else the public official receives
and have that reported as the value of the gift.

     The staff believes that the Chairman's approach makes
accurate reporting very difficult. What is the value of
having a dance band at the event or being able to listen
to a well-known entertainer? The staff also thinks that
the fair market value of the ticket is $100 (since that is
what the price of the ticket is) and that, in accordance
with the statutory requirement of valuing gifts at their
fair market value ($81011), this is the amount that should
be reported.

KF:jo

sections.question: The issue for discussion is how to value the amount of the gift in each of the foregoing situations. Specifically: (1) when a charity fundraiser invites a public official to attend and does not charge him for a ticket, and (2) when a person purchases a ticket to a charity fundraiser and gives it as a gift to a public official.
sections.conclusion: The Commission determined at its meeting on March 3, 1977, that the value of the gift in each of the circumstances is the amount that an individual normally would pay to receive the benefits provided at the event. If the charity fundraiser serves a dinner worth approximately $15, the value of the gift is $15. If it includes entertainment by a well-known celebrity, the value is the amount a person normally would pay to attend a dinner at which the celebrity appeared. To the extent it is difficult to ascertain a precise value, a good faith estimate by the official will suffice.
sections.question_synthetic: How should the value of gifts of charity fundraiser tickets given to public officials be determined—based on the ticket price or the fair market value of actual benefits received (meal, entertainment, etc.)?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC determined that gift value should be calculated as the fair market value of actual benefits received at the event (ticket price or normal admission cost), not the cost of individual components. When precise valuation is difficult, good faith estimates by officials are acceptable.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of two memoranda—the February 23, 1977 memo presenting the question and the March 22, 1977 memo containing the Commission's response/conclusion. No traditional QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections with headers, but clear question and answer content present. Some OCR errors in source document but meaning is discernible.

QUESTION: The issue for discussion is how to value the amount of the gift in each of the foregoing situations. Specifically: (1) when a charity fundraiser invites a public official to attend and does not charge him for a ticket, and (2) when a person purchases a ticket to a charity fundraiser and gives it as a gift to a public official.

CONCLUSION: The Commission determined at its meeting on March 3, 1977, that the value of the gift in each of the circumstances is the amount that an individual normally would pay to receive the benefits provided at the event. If the charity fundraiser serves a dinner worth approximately $15, the value of the gift is $15. If it includes entertainment by a well-known celebrity, the value is the amount a person normally would pay to attend a dinner at which the celebrity appeared. To the extent it is difficult to ascertain a precise value, a good faith estimate by the official will suffice.

embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter addresses how to value gifts of charity fundraiser tickets given to public officials, resolving a dispute between staff (who advocated for ticket price valuation) and the Chairman (who advocated for valuation of actual benefits received). The Commission adopted a fair market value approach based on what a person would normally pay to attend and receive the benefits provided at the event.
